content.problemStatement:
A chain is a series of connected links. David has been presented a chain for his birthday consisting of **N** links and would like to color each of the links into one of **M** colors. He would like the coloring to be pretty uniform, so that no color is used too often or too seldom. Finally, David came with the following restriction: he wants to color the links in such a way that every color is used at least once in every **M**+1 consecutive links.



Two colorings are considered different if there exists at least one **i** between 1 and **N** such that link **i** is colored differently in these colorings. (The links are numbered, and the chain can not be rotated.) How many ways are there for David to color the chain?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers N and M, the length of the chain and the number of colors.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of ways to color the chain modulo 1,000,000,007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one way to color the chain: 1 1. For the second test case, there are 10 ways to color the chain: 1 1, 1 2, 2 1, 2 2, 1 1, 1 2, 2 1, 2 2, 1 2, 2 1. For the third test case, there are 132 ways to color the chain.
There are 150 ways to color the chain of length 5 with 3 colors.
There are 38470 ways to color the chain of length 10 with 5 colors.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We maintain a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to color the first i links such that the last link is colored with the jth color. We iterate over all possible colors for the last link and update dp[i][j] accordingly. We also need to ensure that every color is used at least once in every M+1 consecutive links.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M) per test case, O(T * N * M) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) — for the dp array.
